passages.explanation: It's just before Passover. Jesus knows his time has come to leave this world and return to the Father. John emphasises that Jesus loved his own to the end. During supper, knowing that the Father has given him authority over everything and that he came from God and is going back to God, Jesus gets up, removes his outer garment, wraps a towel around his waist, and begins washing his disciples' feet. This was the job of a household servant — the lowest task in the room. The person with the most authority in the room chose the most menial role.

When Jesus reaches Peter, Peter is appalled — he can't accept his Lord doing a servant's job. Jesus says Peter doesn't understand yet but will later. Peter refuses outright. Jesus responds with a condition: if I don't wash you, you have no part with me. Peter swings to the opposite extreme and asks Jesus to wash his hands and head too. Jesus says that's not necessary — someone who has bathed only needs their feet washed. Then he adds: you are clean, but not all of you. John clarifies that Jesus was referring to Judas, who would betray him.

Jesus sits back down and explains what he just did. If he, their Lord and Teacher, washed their feet, they should wash each other's feet. He's given them an example to follow. A servant is not greater than the master, and a messenger is not greater than the sender. He's not talking about all of them — he knows who he has chosen. He quotes Scripture about someone sharing his bread turning against him. He tells them in advance so that when it happens, they'll believe he is who he says he is. Receiving anyone he sends is the same as receiving him, and receiving him is the same as receiving the Father.

Jesus becomes visibly troubled and says one of them will betray him. The disciples look at each other, confused. The disciple 'whom Jesus loved' — traditionally identified as John — is leaning against Jesus. Peter signals to him to ask who it is. Jesus says it's the one he gives the bread to after dipping it, and hands it to Judas. After Judas takes it, Satan enters him. Jesus tells him: what you're going to do, do quickly. No one else at the table understands what this means — some assume Judas is being sent to buy supplies or give to the poor, since he held the money box. Judas leaves immediately. John's final note on the scene: it was night. The detail functions on two levels — it was literally dark outside, and darkness has symbolised evil and unbelief throughout this gospel.

With Judas gone, Jesus says the Son of Man is now glorified and God is glorified in him. He tells the remaining disciples he'll only be with them a little longer. They can't follow where he's going — the same thing he told the Jewish authorities earlier. Then he gives them a new commandment: love one another the way he has loved them. This is how the world will recognise them as his disciples — not by miracles or teaching, but by how they treat each other.

Peter asks where Jesus is going. Jesus says Peter can't follow now but will later. Peter insists he's ready to die for Jesus. Jesus' response is blunt: before the rooster crows, Peter will deny knowing him three times. Peter's confidence is about to be tested and it will fail.
